title: Gulf states fear Houthi attacks could force their hands | ABC NEWS
description: Houthi rebels in Yemen have vowed to continue attacks against enemies of the Axis of Resistance after their forces launched missiles at Israel.  The militant group’s entry into the war in the Middle East threatens to further expand the conflict as Houthi facilities are better positioned than Tehran to strike US-linked bases in Persian Gulf states. Crucial shipping lanes through the Red Sea are now exposed to the threat of Houthi strikes. title: Lebanese journalists and paramedics killed in separate Israeli strikes | ABC NEWS
description: Three journalists have been killed in an Israeli strike on a media vehicle in southern Lebanon as Israel claims it was targeting a Hezbollah terrorist. Five paramedics have also been killed in an Israeli strike on the Lebanese town of Sulta, as Israeli attacks continue to hit Lebanese health care facilities.  Lebanese Health Minister Rakan Nassereddine is accusing Israel of deliberately targeting Lebanese ambulances and hospitals. title: Anti-Trump protests erupt across US and overseas | ABC NEWS
description: Thousands of ‘No Kings’ protests have erupted in major cities across the United States and Europe.  Demonstrators turned out in large numbers to oppose US President Donald Trump’s war on Iran and immigration policies.  US politicians are closely watching these rallies to gauge public sentiment ahead of the country’s upcoming mid-term elections. title: Will the Houthi rebels create another chokepoint on global energy supply? | ABC NEWS
description: The Houthi rebels in Yemen have opened a third front in the Middle East war after launching strikes at Israel.  The latest escalation of the conflict has sparked fears of Houthi strikes on Arabian Gulf countries and a blockade on a crucial shipping lane in the Red Sea.  Deakin University’s Dr Ali Mamouri says Tehran does not want to use all of its ‘cards’ at once in its struggle against the US and Israel. title: Iran war a 'huge miscalculation' by Trump, says Andrew Hastie | ABC NEWS
description: Coalition frontbencher Andrew Hastie has warned the credibility of the United States is being damaged and says Australians may be losing faith in the alliance as the war in Iran enters its second month.   The shadow minister for industry and sovereign capability said the war in Iran was a "huge miscalculation" and criticised US President Donald Trump's failure to consult its allies, including Australia, noting many were dependent on the export of fuel in and out of the Middle East.  Mr Hastie said as a "close friend" of the US, Australia had a right to "be honest" and ask "hard questions". title: Outback tourist town up in arms over proposed policing plan | ABC NEWS
description: Between the 320-kilometre stretch of highway between Darwin and Katherine, there are only a couple of places to stop for a meal or an overnight stay, one is the historic town of Pine Creek.  The gold rush town has joined a growing list of communities who no longer have a 24/7 police presence and rely on officers from neighboring areas to respond to emergencies.  Residents say a shrinking police presence is already having an impact. Olivana Lathouris has this report for ABC News Northern Territory. title: Leaders hold crisis talks over Australia's fuel supply | ABC NEWS
description: The prime minister will meet with state and territory leaders on Monday in the second emergency National Cabinet meeting since the start of the Middle East war. Much of the focus will be on the pain at the petrol pump and the way that's pushing prices up everywhere. The federal government insists COVID-era restrictions are not on the table, but the states want a co-ordinated approach. title: Dire calls for change as the ambulance service struggles to cope in the NT | ABC NEWS
description: The paramedic in charge of the Northern Territory's ambulance service this weekend, as it reached a point of critical failure, has spoken about the intense pressure being placed on staff.  She says the service is struggling to cope with staff forced to work 12-hour shifts with no breaks. And she's convinced the extraordinary events of this weekend will happen again. Courtney Barrett Peters has this report for ABC News Northern Territory.
